1.synthesis: Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right. 'Honor your father and mother'—this is the first commandment with a promise: 'that it may go well with you and that you may live long in the land' (Ephesians 6:1–3; citing Exodus 20:12). Fathers, do not provoke your children to anger, but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord (Ephesians 6:4). Bondservants, obey your earthly masters with fear and trembling, with a sincere heart, as to Christ, not by way of eye-service as people-pleasers, but as bondservants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart, rendering service with a good will as to the Lord and not to man, knowing that whatever good anyone does, this he will receive back from the Lord, whether he is a bondservant or free (Ephesians 6:5–8). Masters, do the same to them, and stop threatening, knowing that he who is both their Master and yours is in heaven, and that there is no partiality with him (Ephesians 6:9).
1.historical_context: The Decalogue citation (Exodus 20:12; Deuteronomy 5:16) applies the fifth commandment directly to the Ephesian community—both Jewish and Gentile believers are called to honor parents, grounding the command in the Torah that has been fulfilled in Christ. 'Do not provoke your children to anger' (v.4) is the counterbalancing command to fathers: the Greco-Roman paterfamilias had virtually absolute authority over children; Paul limits this with the obligation not to provoke. 'Bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord' (v.4) makes the Lord, not the father's own preferences, the standard of child-rearing. The slave-master section (vv.5–9) is the most extensive in the household codes—reflecting the reality that the early church included significant numbers of slaves. Paul does not condemn the institution (a social structure beyond any individual's power to change) but transforms the relationship: slaves serve 'as to Christ,' masters acknowledge 'no partiality with God'—both parties stand before the same Lord who will render judgment without regard to social status.
1.literary_structure: Each pair in the household code receives asymmetric commands: children obey; fathers do not provoke but nurture in the Lord's instruction. Slaves serve as to the Lord; masters do the same and stop threatening, knowing God's impartiality. In each case the socially subordinate party receives the instruction to serve within the existing structure, and the socially dominant party receives the instruction to exercise authority in a way that serves rather than exploits. The christological ground appears throughout: 'in the Lord' (v.1), 'of the Lord' (v.4), 'as to Christ' (v.5), 'as bondservants of Christ' (v.6), 'as to the Lord' (v.7), 'the Lord will reward' (v.8), 'he who is both their Master and yours is in heaven' (v.9). Christ is the real referent of every relationship in the household.
1.theological_application: The 'no partiality with God' principle (v.9) is the deepest social implication of the household code: the same Lord judges master and slave, parent and child, with equal standard. This does not immediately abolish social hierarchies but fundamentally relativizes them: the master who threatens his slave is answerable to the same heavenly Master who requires no threatening of those he rules. The children-obey/fathers-nurture pair models the integration of appropriate authority with appropriate responsibility: authority without nurture is tyranny; nurture without the structure of respectful obedience is formlessness. 'Bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord' (v.4) makes Christian formation the purpose of parental authority—the goal of family structure is the formation of members of the Lord's community, not the consolidation of parental power.
1.christology: The repeated 'as to the Lord/as to Christ' in the slave-master section transforms every act of obedience and every exercise of authority into a theological act: the slave who serves a human master 'as to Christ' is serving Christ through the master; the master who treats slaves as one who has a Master in heaven recognizes Christ's lordship over his own authority. The 'no partiality' principle (v.9) is a christological statement: the Christ who 'became poor so that others might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9), who abolished the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile (Ephesians 2:14), is not interested in the social distinctions that give masters power over slaves. The eschatological 'the Lord will reward each one for whatever good he does' (v.8) democratizes the moral economy: no human hierarchy determines ultimate standing before the Lord who judges impartially.

10.synthesis: Finally, be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might. Put on the whole armor of God, that they may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil. For they do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness, against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places (Ephesians 6:10–12). Therefore take up the whole armor of God, that they may be able to withstand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand firm. Stand therefore, having fastened on the belt of truth, and having put on the breastplate of righteousness, and having shod their feet with the readiness given by the gospel of peace. In all circumstances take up the shield of faith, with which they can extinguish all the flaming darts of the evil one; and take the helmet of salvation, and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God, praying at all times in the Spirit, with all prayer and supplication (Ephesians 6:13–18). They are to keep alert with all perseverance, making supplication for all the saints, and also for Paul, that words may be given to him in opening his mouth boldly to proclaim the mystery of the gospel, for which he is an ambassador in chains (Ephesians 6:19–20).
10.historical_context: The spiritual warfare passage draws on Isaiah's descriptions of God's own armament (Isaiah 11:5; 59:17: 'he put on righteousness as a breastplate, and a helmet of salvation on his head')—the armor that Paul commands believers to put on is the armor of God himself, not merely human equipment. This is crucial: the armor does not originate with the soldier but with the God who provides it. The 'rulers, authorities, cosmic powers, spiritual forces of evil in heavenly places' (v.12) reflect the letter's developed angelology/demonology: Ephesians has the most developed account in the NT of spiritual powers arrayed against the church (cf. 1:21; 2:2; 3:10). Paul writes from prison ('ambassador in chains,' v.20), making his request for bold proclamation all the more poignant: the imprisoned apostle asks prayer for the open mouth that chains cannot close. The six armor pieces (belt, breastplate, shoes, shield, helmet, sword) correspond generally to standard Roman soldier's equipment, making the imagery immediately recognizable in the military culture of the empire.
10.literary_structure: The armor passage is the letter's climactic section, drawing together its major theological themes—the cosmic scope of Christ's lordship (1:20–21), the spiritual warfare already won in the heavenlies (2:6), and the community's calling to stand in what Christ has accomplished. The six pieces of armor are not six separate practices but a unified description of the Christian's equipped standing in Christ: truth (integrity), righteousness (Christ's standing), gospel of peace (proclamation readiness), faith (trust in God's protection), salvation (the accomplished work), and the word of God (the Spirit's weapon). Prayer (v.18) is not the seventh piece but the atmosphere in which all the armor is worn—the ongoing communication with the Commander that makes the soldier effective. The personal prayer request (vv.19–20) grounds the cosmic vision in Paul's concrete situation: the imprisoned apostle who has described the church's heavenly position is himself in chains, needing prayer for bold proclamation.
10.theological_application: The armor of God passage reframes the entire Christian life as spiritual warfare—not against human opponents ('not against flesh and blood,' v.12) but against the structural spiritual forces that drive human opposition to God and God's people. This means that the community's primary battles are not political, social, or institutional but spiritual: the same powers that the ascended Christ has subjected (1:21) continue to operate 'in this present darkness' (v.12), and the community's role is to 'stand'—not to advance on new territory but to hold the ground that Christ has already won. The armor's defensive character (five pieces protect; only the sword and the word of prayer are used offensively) corresponds to this standing posture: the battle has been won; the community's task is to maintain position in the victory already accomplished. Prayer 'at all times in the Spirit' (v.18) is the action that holds all the armor in place—the ongoing communion with the Commander-in-Chief.
10.christology: The armor that believers put on is God's own armor (Isaiah 59:17), and in the context of Ephesians, God's armor has been worn by the incarnate Son—he is the one who girded himself with truth, put on righteousness as breastplate, brought the gospel of peace, exercised faith/faithfulness unto death, wore the helmet of salvation in his obedient death, and wielded the word of God against Satan's temptations (Matthew 4:1–11). When believers 'put on the armor of God,' they are putting on what Christ already wears—participating in his own victory over the powers. 'The sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God' (v.17) is the Spirit's instrument, not primarily the believer's: the Spirit wields the word through the believer's proclamation as the weapon that brings the powers' deceptions into the light of the gospel's truth. Paul's prayer for boldness (v.20) is ultimately a prayer that the Spirit will wield this sword through his imprisoned servant.

21.synthesis: Tychicus, the beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord, will make known to them everything, so that they also may know how Paul is and what he is doing. Paul is sending him for this very purpose, that they may know how he is and that Tychicus may encourage their hearts (Ephesians 6:21–22). Peace be to the brothers, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. Grace be with all who love our Lord Jesus Christ with love incorruptible (Ephesians 6:23–24).
21.historical_context: Tychicus is one of Paul's most trusted co-workers, identified as a companion in Acts 20:4 and mentioned also in Colossians 4:7–8, 2 Timothy 4:12, and Titus 3:12. The near-identical commissioning of Tychicus in Ephesians 6:21–22 and Colossians 4:7–8 has led many scholars to conclude that Ephesians was a circular letter intended for multiple congregations (some manuscripts lack 'in Ephesus' in 1:1), with Tychicus as the carrier of both letters. The final benediction of vv.23–24 is unusually formal—'peace to the brothers' rather than the typical Pauline 'grace to you'—which also supports the circular-letter hypothesis: the letter is addressed to a wider community than a single well-known congregation. 'Love incorruptible' (en aphtharsia, v.24) is the same word used in 1 Corinthians 15 for the resurrection body—the love with which believers love Christ is characterized by the same imperishability that marks the resurrection.
21.literary_structure: The letter closes with a personal commendation (Tychicus, vv.21–22) and a double benediction (peace and love with faith, vv.23; grace for those who love incorruptibly, v.24). The structure mirrors the opening: 'grace and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ' (1:2) is answered by 'peace to the brothers... grace with all who love our Lord Jesus Christ' (6:23–24). The letter that opened with 'every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ' (1:3) closes with peace, love, faith, and grace—the experienced realities of those blessings in the community's common life. The final phrase 'with love incorruptible' ties the letter's eschatological vision (resurrection, the fullness of Christ, the heavenly places) to the community's present experience: loving the Lord Jesus Christ with an undying love is the already-present form of the imperishable life that awaits the new creation.
21.theological_application: The closing benediction—'grace be with all who love our Lord Jesus Christ with love incorruptible' (v.24)—closes the letter with the criterion of genuine community membership: not circumcision, not ethnic identity, not social status, but love for the Lord Jesus Christ that has the character of incorruptibility. This is the letter's final answer to the question of who belongs to the community of blessing: all who love the Lord Jesus Christ—Jew and Gentile, slave and free, male and female—with the undying love that is the Spirit's fruit (5:22: 'love' is the first fruit of the Spirit). The peace and love with faith that Paul pronounces (v.23) are the three theological virtues (faith, love, peace) that frame the entire ethical argument of the letter: faith in the one who has accomplished all things, love that flows from his love, and peace that results from reconciliation to God and to one another through the cross.
21.christology: 'Peace to the brothers, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ' (v.23)—the peace and love flow jointly from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ: the same joint source as the letter's opening (1:2) and its central theological claim (2:14: 'he himself is our peace'). The letter that began with the eternal purpose of God the Father to gather all things in Christ (1:10) closes with the grace given to those who love the Lord Jesus Christ with undying love. The entire letter is a meditation on what it means for everything to be gathered up in Christ—the cosmic Christ who is head over all things for the church (1:22), the reconciling Christ who broke down the dividing wall (2:14), the mystery Christ in whom the nations are fellow heirs (3:6), and the Lord whose armor the community wears as it stands against the powers until the consummation when God will be all in all.
